articles.headline: National Logistics Policy Phase II Kicks Off, Aims for Seamless Supply Chains and Reduced Costs
articles.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Commerce and Industry announced the commencement of Phase II of the National Logistics Policy (NLP).
• This phase focuses on integrating advanced technologies like AI and blockchain, enhancing multi-modal connectivity, and streamlining regulatory frameworks.
• The primary objective is to reduce logistics costs from the current 13-14% of GDP to a global benchmark of 8% by 2030.
• Key initiatives include developing a national logistics portal for real-time tracking, promoting green logistics, and upskilling the logistics workforce.
• The policy seeks to boost India's competitiveness in global trade and improve ease of doing business for various sectors.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: National Logistics Policy (NLP) launched in 2022.
Aims to reduce logistics cost to ~8% of GDP.
Phase II focuses on technology integration (AI, blockchain) and green logistics.
PM Gati Shakti is a complementary initiative for integrated infrastructure planning.
Unified Logistics Interface Platform (ULIP) is a digital tool under NLP.
mainsPoints: Impact on economic competitiveness and export growth: Reduced costs make Indian goods more competitive internationally.
Role in Make in India: Efficient logistics support domestic manufacturing and supply chains.
Challenges: Land acquisition, inter-state coordination, last-mile connectivity, skill development, and digital infrastructure gaps.
Environmental sustainability: Promotion of green logistics, electric vehicles, and efficient warehousing can reduce carbon footprint.
Employment generation: Creation of jobs in warehousing, transportation, and technology-driven logistics services.
articles.imageDescription: A graphic depicting various modes of transport (road, rail, air, water) integrated into a seamless logistics network.
articles.trivia: India's logistics sector is one of the largest employers, providing livelihoods to millions, and is projected to grow significantly with policy support.

articles.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Provisions of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 with Clarifications
articles.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, upheld the constitutional validity of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023.
• The court clarified certain provisions regarding data fiduciaries' obligations and the balance between individual privacy and state access to data.
• Emphasized the importance of a robust grievance redressal mechanism and the independent functioning of the Data Protection Board of India.
• The judgment provided guidelines for 'significant data fiduciaries' and the conditions under which personal data can be processed.
• This ruling is expected to provide much-needed legal clarity and strengthen India's data governance framework.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections
keyTerms: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act)
prelimsPoints: DPDP Act, 2023 replaced the Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019.
Defines 'Data Principal' as the individual whose data is processed and 'Data Fiduciary' as the entity determining the purpose and means of processing.
Establishes the Data Protection Board of India as an independent regulatory body.
Based on principles of purpose limitation and data minimization.
Introduces significant penalties for non-compliance.
mainsPoints: Implications for individual privacy: Strengthens individual rights over personal data, enhances consent mechanisms, and provides avenues for grievance redressal.
Balance between privacy and state surveillance: The court's clarifications address concerns about exemptions for government agencies, emphasizing proportionality and necessity.
Impact on digital economy: Provides a clear legal framework for businesses, fostering trust and enabling growth in India's digital sector.
Role of Data Protection Board: Crucial for effective enforcement, investigation of breaches, and imposing penalties.
Comparison with global standards: Aligns India's data protection regime with international best practices like GDPR, facilitating cross-border data flows.
articles.imageDescription: A gavel and a digital screen showing binary code, symbolizing law and digital data.
articles.trivia: India's journey towards a comprehensive data protection law began with the Puttaswamy judgment (2017), which declared privacy a fundamental right.

articles.headline: India Pledges Significant Contribution to Global South Climate Adaptation Fund at UN Meet
articles.explainer:
• India announced a substantial financial commitment to the newly established 'Global South Climate Adaptation Fund' during a high-level UN climate summit.
• The fund aims to support developing nations in building resilience against the adverse impacts of climate change, focusing on vulnerable communities.
• India's pledge underscores its leadership role in advocating for climate justice and equitable burden-sharing in climate finance.
• The initiative is expected to facilitate technology transfer, capacity building, and implementation of adaptation projects in climate-vulnerable regions.
• This move aligns with India's long-standing call for developed nations to fulfill their climate finance commitments.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: Global South refers to developing countries, often in Asia, Africa, and Latin America.
Climate adaptation involves adjusting to actual or expected future climate.
Climate finance is funding from developed to developing countries for climate action.
India is a strong proponent of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC).
The fund aims to address the disproportionate impact of climate change on vulnerable nations.
mainsPoints: India's leadership in Global South: Reinforces India's role as a voice for developing nations and a leader in sustainable development.
Addressing climate justice: Highlights the ethical imperative of supporting countries least responsible for climate change but most affected.
Impact on international climate negotiations: Puts pressure on developed nations to increase their climate finance contributions and fulfill commitments.
Geopolitical implications: Enhances India's soft power and diplomatic influence among developing countries.
Challenges in fund utilization: Ensuring transparency, effective project implementation, and equitable distribution of resources remain crucial.
articles.imageDescription: A world map highlighting the Global South, with symbols representing climate change impacts and adaptation efforts.
articles.trivia: The concept of 'Loss and Damage' in climate change refers to the irreversible impacts of climate change that go beyond what adaptation can achieve.

articles.headline: Project Lion 2.0 Launched, Focus on Habitat Expansion, Community Involvement, and Disease Management
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change launched 'Project Lion 2.0' as an upgraded initiative for the conservation of Asiatic Lions.
• The new phase emphasizes expanding the lion's habitat beyond Gir National Park, exploring potential translocation sites.
• Significant focus is placed on enhancing community participation through local livelihood generation and awareness programs.
• Advanced disease management protocols, including genomic studies and vaccination drives, are integral to protecting the lion population from epidemics.
• The project aims for a holistic approach to ensure the long-term survival and genetic viability of the Asiatic Lion.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: Asiatic Lions are listed as 'Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
Their only wild population exists in Gujarat's Gir National Park and its surrounding areas.
Project Lion was initially announced in 2020.
Disease outbreaks (e.g., Canine Distemper Virus) pose a significant threat to the concentrated population.
Translocation to a second home is a key strategy to create a metapopulation and mitigate risks.
mainsPoints: Importance of habitat expansion: Reduces risk of disease, genetic bottleneck, and human-wildlife conflict in a single, concentrated population.
Role of community involvement: Essential for long-term conservation success, reducing poaching, and ensuring local support for conservation efforts through ecotourism and awareness.
Disease management strategies: Genomic studies help understand susceptibility, while vaccination and rapid response teams are crucial for preventing epidemics.
Challenges in translocation: Identifying suitable habitats, ensuring prey base, managing human-lion interactions in new areas, and political will.
Broader implications for biodiversity: Successful lion conservation sets a precedent for other endangered species and highlights India's commitment to biodiversity.
articles.imageDescription: An Asiatic Lion resting in its natural habitat, with a backdrop of dry deciduous forest.
articles.trivia: The Asiatic Lion is slightly smaller than its African cousin and has a less developed mane in males, often revealing their ears.

articles.headline: India's Manufacturing PMI Sustains Strong Growth in December, Signals Robust Economic Momentum
articles.explainer:
• The S&P Global India Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) for December 2025 indicated sustained expansion in the sector.
• The index remained well above the 50-point mark, signaling continued improvement in operating conditions.
• Key drivers included robust new orders, increased production, and a positive outlook for future output.
• This consistent growth reflects strong domestic demand and a gradual recovery in export orders.
• The data suggests a healthy start to the new year for India's industrial sector, underpinning overall economic resilience.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
prelimsPoints: PMI is a survey-based indicator of economic trends in manufacturing and services sectors.
A reading above 50 indicates expansion, while below 50 indicates contraction.
It covers new orders, output, employment, suppliers' delivery times, and stocks of purchases.
PMI data is released monthly by S&P Global (earlier IHS Markit).
It is considered a leading indicator of economic health.
articles.imageDescription: A bar chart showing the trend of India's Manufacturing PMI over several months, indicating growth.
articles.trivia: Globally, PMI data is closely watched by investors and policymakers as an early indicator of economic shifts, often influencing central bank decisions.

articles.headline: ISRO Gears Up for Chandrayaan-4 Lunar Lander Mission in Q2 2026, Focus on Polar Region Exploration
articles.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced accelerated preparations for its ambitious Chandrayaan-4 mission, slated for Q2 2026.
• This mission is envisioned as a lunar lander and rover mission, specifically targeting the Moon's south polar region.
• The primary objectives include in-situ analysis of lunar soil and rocks, searching for water ice, and studying the Moon's exosphere.
• Chandrayaan-4 will build upon the successes of Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3, incorporating advanced navigation and hazard avoidance systems.
• This mission reinforces India's growing capabilities in lunar exploration and its commitment to space science.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: Chandrayaan-1 (2008) confirmed water molecules on the Moon.
Chandrayaan-2 (2019) included an orbiter, lander (Vikram), and rover (Pragyan); lander soft-landing failed.
Chandrayaan-3 (2023) successfully soft-landed Vikram and deployed Pragyan near the lunar south pole.
Moon's south polar region is of high scientific interest due to potential water ice reserves in permanently shadowed craters.
Chandrayaan-4 aims for more advanced in-situ analysis.
mainsPoints: Scientific significance: Understanding lunar geology, evolution, and potential resources, especially water ice, crucial for future human missions.
Technological advancements: Showcases India's prowess in precision landing, autonomous navigation, and robotic exploration in deep space.
Geopolitical implications: Enhances India's standing as a major space power, fosters international collaborations (e.g., with Artemis Accords).
Economic potential: Development of technologies with spin-off benefits for terrestrial applications and growth of India's private space sector.
Inspiration for youth: Motivates young generations towards STEM fields and scientific inquiry, contributing to human capital development.
articles.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the Chandrayaan-4 lunar lander and rover on the Moon's surface, with the Earth in the background.
articles.trivia: The Moon's south pole region is considered a strategic location for future lunar bases due to the presence of water ice, which can be converted into rocket fuel and breathable air.

articles.headline: NEP Implementation Review Highlights Persistent Digital Divide in Access to Quality Education
articles.explainer:
• A comprehensive review of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's implementation revealed significant challenges, particularly concerning the digital divide.
• While digital learning initiatives have expanded, disparities in access to devices, internet connectivity, and digital literacy persist, especially in rural and disadvantaged areas.
• The review highlighted that a substantial portion of students and educators still lack adequate infrastructure to fully leverage online educational resources.
• Recommendations include targeted funding for digital infrastructure in underserved regions, training programs for teachers, and development of accessible digital content.
• The findings underscore the need for equitable access to technology to achieve the NEP's vision of inclusive and quality education for all.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry – the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
mainsPoints: Challenges of digital divide: Lack of devices, internet access, electricity, and digital literacy in rural/poor urban areas exacerbates educational inequalities.
Impact on NEP goals: Hinders the implementation of blended learning, online courses, and digital skill development envisioned by NEP.
Government initiatives: Programs like Digital India, BharatNet, and DIKSHA are crucial but require further reach and effectiveness.
Policy recommendations: Need for public-private partnerships, community-based digital learning centers, subsidized devices, and vernacular digital content.
Role of teachers: Training teachers in digital pedagogy and providing technical support is vital for effective integration of technology in classrooms.
articles.imageDescription: A split image showing a student using a tablet in a modern classroom versus a student struggling with limited resources in a rural setting.
articles.trivia: The National Education Policy 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India, replacing the 1986 policy.

articles.headline: India-Nepal Border Management Talks Conclude, Emphasizing Joint Patrolling and Information Sharing
articles.explainer:
• High-level talks between India and Nepal on border management concluded with a joint statement emphasizing enhanced cooperation.
• Both nations agreed to intensify joint patrolling along the open border and increase real-time intelligence sharing to combat cross-border crime.
• Discussions also covered improving border infrastructure, facilitating legitimate trade, and addressing concerns related to illegal activities.
• The meeting highlighted the shared commitment to maintaining peace and stability along the porous border, which is crucial for bilateral relations.
• This dialogue is part of ongoing efforts to strengthen security mechanisms and foster mutual trust between the two South Asian neighbors.
gsSubTopics: Border areas management
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: India shares a 1,751 km long open border with Nepal.
Sashastra Seema Bal (SSB) is India's primary border guarding force for India-Nepal and India-Bhutan borders.
The open border facilitates movement of people but also poses security challenges (smuggling, human trafficking).
The 1950 Treaty of Peace and Friendship governs India-Nepal relations, including open borders.
Regular bilateral mechanisms exist for security and border management discussions.
mainsPoints: Challenges of an open border: Facilitates illegal activities like smuggling of arms, drugs, human trafficking, and movement of anti-India elements.
Importance of cooperation: Joint patrolling and intelligence sharing are vital for effective border management and combating transnational crime.
Impact on bilateral relations: Border issues, if not managed cooperatively, can strain diplomatic ties and lead to public discontent.
Role of border infrastructure: Development of integrated check posts (ICPs) and better connectivity can streamline legitimate trade and movement.
Addressing local concerns: Ensuring that security measures do not unduly hamper local livelihoods and traditional cross-border interactions is crucial.
articles.imageDescription: A map showing the India-Nepal border, with symbols indicating joint patrols and security checkpoints.
articles.trivia: The India-Nepal border is unique as it is largely an 'open border', allowing free movement of people without passports or visas.

articles.headline: Government Accelerates Disinvestment Drive, Targets Strategic Sale of Key PSUs by March End
articles.explainer:
• The Union government announced an accelerated disinvestment plan, aiming to complete the strategic sale of several Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) by the end of the current fiscal year (March 2026).
• The focus is on non-strategic PSUs and minority stake sales in others to meet fiscal targets and unlock capital for public welfare schemes.
• This move signals the government's commitment to reducing its presence in non-core sectors and improving efficiency through private sector participation.
• Potential candidates for disinvestment include entities in banking, insurance, and manufacturing sectors.
• The proceeds from these sales are crucial for managing the fiscal deficit and funding infrastructure projects.
gsSubTopics: Government Budgeting
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: Disinvestment is the process of government selling its assets or a part of it, usually shares of PSUs.
Strategic disinvestment involves selling a majority stake along with transfer of management control.
The Department of Investment and Public Asset Management (DIPAM) is responsible for disinvestment.
Objectives include promoting private ownership, reducing fiscal burden, and generating revenue.
Non-strategic sectors are those where the government plans to exit completely.
mainsPoints: Rationale for disinvestment: Improves efficiency, brings in private capital and technology, reduces government's fiscal burden, and allows focus on core governance functions.
Challenges: Valuation issues, labor union resistance, political opposition, market conditions, and ensuring transparency in the sale process.
Impact on economy: Can boost investor confidence, attract foreign investment, and improve corporate governance in privatized entities.
Utilization of proceeds: Funds can be used for infrastructure development, social sector spending, or debt reduction.
Strategic vs. Non-strategic sectors: Government policy outlines sectors where public presence is deemed essential (strategic) versus those open for full privatization (non-strategic).
articles.imageDescription: A chart showing the government's disinvestment targets and achievements over recent years.
articles.trivia: The first major wave of disinvestment in India began in the early 1990s as part of economic liberalization reforms.

articles.headline: India Exceeds 2025 Renewable Energy Target, Shifts Focus to Grid Modernization and Storage Solutions
articles.explainer:
• India has successfully surpassed its ambitious renewable energy capacity target set for 2025, a year ahead of schedule.
• The country's installed renewable capacity (excluding large hydro) has crossed the 200 GW mark, largely driven by solar and wind power.
• With this achievement, the focus is now shifting towards critical next-generation challenges: grid integration, energy storage, and ensuring grid stability.
• New policies are being formulated to promote utility-scale battery storage, pumped hydro projects, and smart grid technologies.
• This milestone significantly contributes to India's climate goals and its commitment to a sustainable energy transition.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
articles.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: India's initial target was 175 GW of renewable energy by 2022, later revised and expanded.
Solar and wind power constitute the largest share of India's renewable energy mix.
Challenges of renewable energy include intermittency and grid stability issues.
Energy storage solutions (e.g., batteries, pumped hydro) are crucial for balancing the grid.
India is a founding member of the International Solar Alliance (ISA).
mainsPoints: Significance of achievement: Demonstrates India's commitment to climate action, reduces reliance on fossil fuels, and enhances energy security.
Challenges of grid integration: Managing intermittency, ensuring stable supply, and upgrading transmission infrastructure to handle fluctuating renewable input.
Importance of energy storage: Essential for firming up renewable power, shifting generation to peak demand, and providing grid services.
Policy measures for future: Need for clear long-term policies, incentives for storage technologies, smart grid development, and green hydrogen integration.
Economic and environmental benefits: Creates green jobs, reduces air pollution, and contributes to India's global leadership in renewable energy.
articles.imageDescription: A vast solar power farm stretching to the horizon, with a few wind turbines in the distance.
articles.trivia: Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan is one of the world's largest solar parks, with a capacity of over 2.2 GW.

articles.headline: Election Commission of India Proposes New Guidelines for Transparency in Online Political Campaigning
articles.explainer:
• The Election Commission of India (ECI) has put forth a series of proposed guidelines aimed at increasing transparency and accountability in online political campaigning.
• These guidelines address issues such as undisclosed political advertisements, use of deepfakes, and foreign interference in electoral processes.
• Key proposals include mandatory disclosure of funding sources for online political ads and clear labeling of AI-generated content.
• The ECI seeks to engage with political parties, social media platforms, and civil society for feedback before finalizing the framework.
• This initiative is crucial for safeguarding electoral integrity in the digital age and ensuring a level playing field.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
prelimsPoints: ECI is an autonomous constitutional body responsible for administering elections in India (Article 324).
Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is a set of guidelines issued by the ECI for political parties and candidates.
Deepfakes are synthetic media in which a person in an existing image or video is replaced with someone else's likeness.
RPA 1951 governs the conduct of elections.
ECI has been pushing for reforms to address challenges posed by social media.
mainsPoints: Challenges of online campaigning: Spread of misinformation, hate speech, deepfakes, lack of accountability for anonymous content, and foreign influence.
Importance of transparency: Ensures voters are aware of the sources and funding behind political messages, fostering informed decision-making.
Role of ECI: Essential for adapting electoral laws to the digital age, maintaining fairness, and protecting the integrity of democratic processes.
Impact on free speech vs. regulation: Balancing freedom of expression with the need to prevent manipulation and ensure a healthy public discourse.
Technological solutions: Collaboration with social media platforms, use of AI for content moderation, and digital forensics to detect violations.
articles.imageDescription: A stylized image of a ballot box overlaid with social media icons and network lines, representing digital election challenges.
articles.trivia: India uses Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs) for general and state assembly elections, first adopted extensively in 2004.

articles.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Critical Phase Amidst Key Sticking Points
articles.explainer:
• Negotiations for the proposed India-European Union Free Trade Agreement (FTA) have entered a crucial and intensive phase.
• While significant progress has been made on several chapters, key sticking points remain, particularly concerning market access for goods, intellectual property rights, and sustainable development provisions.
• Both sides are working to bridge differences and find common ground to finalize the comprehensive agreement.
• A successful FTA is expected to boost bilateral trade and investment, creating new opportunities for businesses in both regions.
• The agreement aims to reduce tariffs, ease non-tariff barriers, and establish common rules for trade and investment.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
FTA aims to eliminate or reduce tariffs, making trade cheaper.
Non-tariff barriers include regulations, standards, and licensing requirements.
IPR protection is a key concern for the EU, while India seeks greater market access for its services.
Negotiations for the FTA started in 2007, paused, and resumed in 2022.
mainsPoints: Economic benefits for India: Increased exports, access to advanced technology, boost to manufacturing, and greater foreign direct investment.
Challenges for India: Competition for domestic industries, stringent EU regulations (e.g., environmental, labor standards), and potential impact on pharmaceutical sector due to IPR provisions.
Geopolitical significance: Strengthens strategic partnership between India and EU, providing a counterweight to other global powers.
Sticking points: EU's demand for deeper tariff cuts on certain goods (e.g., automobiles, wines), India's concerns on data localization, and differences over labor and environmental standards.
Way forward: Requires flexibility and pragmatic approaches from both sides, focusing on mutually beneficial outcomes rather than zero-sum gains.
articles.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union intertwined, symbolizing diplomatic and trade relations.
articles.trivia: The EU is a political and economic union of 27 member states located primarily in Europe.

articles.headline: Indian Researchers Develop Promising Indigenous mRNA Vaccine for Neglected Tropical Diseases
articles.explainer:
• A team of Indian scientists, backed by the Department of Biotechnology, has announced a breakthrough in developing an indigenous mRNA vaccine.
• The vaccine targets a cluster of Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) prevalent in endemic regions, offering a cost-effective solution.
• Early clinical trial results have shown promising efficacy and safety profiles, paving the way for larger-scale trials.
• This development highlights India's growing capabilities in advanced vaccine technology and its commitment to global health equity.
• The initiative aims to reduce the burden of NTDs, which disproportionately affect vulnerable populations in low-income settings.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: mRNA vaccines work by teaching our cells how to make a protein (or even just a piece of a protein) that triggers an immune response.
Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) are a diverse group of communicable diseases that prevail in tropical and subtropical conditions.
Examples of NTDs include dengue, chikungunya, kala-azar, lymphatic filariasis, leprosy, and rabies.
India bears a significant burden of NTDs.
Indigenous vaccine development enhances self-reliance and accessibility.
mainsPoints: Significance for public health: Addresses diseases affecting vulnerable populations, reduces morbidity and mortality, and improves quality of life.
Technological self-reliance: Demonstrates India's capability in cutting-edge biotechnological research, reducing dependence on foreign manufacturers.
Economic impact: Reduces healthcare expenditure, prevents loss of productivity due to illness, and fosters innovation in the pharmaceutical sector.
Global health equity: Provides affordable vaccine solutions for developing countries, aligning with India's role as a 'pharmacy of the world'.
Challenges in deployment: Ensuring cold chain infrastructure, community acceptance, and equitable distribution in remote areas.
articles.imageDescription: A microscopic view of mRNA strands, with a representation of a vaccine vial.
articles.trivia: The first mRNA vaccines approved for human use were for COVID-19, marking a new era in vaccinology.

articles.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Significant Buddhist Monastery Remains in Odisha
articles.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of extensive remains of an ancient Buddhist monastery in Odisha's Jajpur district.
• The excavation has revealed several stupas, prayer halls, and monastic cells, along with intricate sculptures and inscriptions dating back to the Gupta and post-Gupta periods.
• This finding adds to the rich Buddhist heritage of Odisha, which was once a prominent center for Buddhist learning and art.
• The site is expected to shed new light on the spread of Buddhism in eastern India and its architectural evolution.
• Conservation efforts are underway, and the site is being prepared for public access and further research.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage.
Odisha has a rich Buddhist past, with sites like Ratnagiri, Lalitgiri, and Udayagiri (Diamond Triangle).
Stupas are mound-like structures containing relics, while Viharas are monastic complexes.
Gupta period (c. 320 to 550 CE) is often referred to as the 'Golden Age' of India, known for advancements in art, science, and literature.
Jajpur district in Odisha is known for its archaeological significance.
mainsPoints: Significance of the discovery: Provides new evidence for the spread and architectural styles of Buddhism in Eastern India, enriching historical understanding.
Contribution to cultural tourism: Attracts national and international tourists, boosting local economy and promoting cultural exchange.
Challenges in conservation: Protecting the delicate remains from natural elements, encroachment, and ensuring sustainable tourism development.
Role of ASI: Crucial for scientific excavation, preservation, and interpretation of India's vast archaeological heritage.
Impact on regional history: May necessitate revision of historical narratives regarding the patronage and influence of Buddhist centers in Odisha.
articles.imageDescription: Archaeological excavation site showing remnants of ancient brick structures and a stone sculpture.
articles.trivia: The 'Diamond Triangle' of Odisha (Lalitgiri, Ratnagiri, Udayagiri) is a cluster of Buddhist sites known for their ancient monasteries and stupas.

articles.headline: Smart Cities Mission 3.0 to Prioritize Climate-Resilient Urban Infrastructure and Green Mobility
articles.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced the conceptualization of 'Smart Cities Mission 3.0', with a strong emphasis on climate resilience.
• The next phase will focus on integrating sustainable practices, green infrastructure, and climate-adaptive urban planning into city development.
• Key areas include developing robust drainage systems, promoting renewable energy in urban spaces, and expanding electric vehicle charging infrastructure.
• The mission aims to make Indian cities more sustainable, livable, and prepared for the challenges posed by climate change.
• It will also encourage citizen participation in urban planning and digital governance for improved city services.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
articles.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission was launched in 2015.
Aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure, a clean and sustainable environment, and application of 'Smart' solutions.
Climate resilience refers to the ability of a system to anticipate, absorb, accommodate, or recover from the effects of a hazardous event.
Green mobility includes electric vehicles, public transport, and non-motorized transport.
Digital governance involves using ICT for efficient and transparent city management.
mainsPoints: Necessity of climate resilience: Indian cities are vulnerable to extreme weather events (floods, heatwaves), necessitating adaptive infrastructure and planning.
Components of green infrastructure: Rainwater harvesting, urban forests, green buildings, and sustainable waste management contribute to city resilience.
Challenges in implementation: Funding constraints, inter-agency coordination, citizen engagement, and ensuring equitable development across all city areas.
Role of technology: Smart sensors, data analytics, and AI can optimize resource management, predict disasters, and enhance urban services.
Social equity: Ensuring that climate-resilient initiatives benefit all sections of society, especially the urban poor, and do not exacerbate existing inequalities.
articles.imageDescription: A futuristic city skyline with green spaces, electric vehicles, and smart buildings, symbolizing a 'smart city'.
articles.trivia: The Smart Cities Mission aims to develop 100 smart cities across India.

highlights: National Logistics Policy Phase II launched to enhance supply chain efficiency and reduce costs, targeting 8% of GDP by 2030.
Supreme Court upholds key provisions of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023, providing legal clarity on data governance.
India pledges significant contribution to the Global South Climate Adaptation Fund, affirming leadership in climate justice.
Project Lion 2.0 initiated with a focus on habitat expansion, community involvement, and disease management for Asiatic Lions.
ISRO gears up for Chandrayaan-4 lunar lander mission in Q2 2026, targeting the Moon's south polar region for advanced exploration.

prelimsMocks.question:
With reference to the National Logistics Policy (NLP) of India, consider the following statements:
1. The policy aims to reduce logistics costs to 8% of GDP by 2030.
2. PM Gati Shakti is an umbrella initiative for integrated infrastructure development that complements the NLP.
3. The Unified Logistics Interface Platform (ULIP) is a digital portal designed to provide real-time information and services under NLP.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. The National Logistics Policy (NLP) indeed aims to reduce logistics costs to a global benchmark of 8% of GDP by 2030. PM Gati Shakti is an overarching initiative for integrated planning and coordinated implementation of infrastructure projects, directly supporting the NLP's goals. ULIP is a key digital component of NLP, facilitating seamless information exchange and logistics services.

prelimsMocks.question:
Which of the following statements about the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023, is/are correct?
1. It defines 'Data Principal' as the individual whose data is processed and 'Data Fiduciary' as the entity determining the purpose and means of processing.
2. It establishes the Data Protection Board of India as an independent regulatory body.
3. It is based on the principles of purpose limitation and data minimization.
Select the correct answer using the code given below:
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct regarding the DPDP Act, 2023. The Act clearly defines Data Principal and Data Fiduciary, establishes the Data Protection Board of India for enforcement, and is founded on core principles like purpose limitation (data collected only for specified purposes) and data minimization (collecting only necessary data).

prelimsMocks.question:
Consider the following statements regarding Project Lion 2.0:
1. It aims to expand the habitat of Asiatic Lions beyond Gir National Park.
2. It emphasizes community participation and livelihood generation for local populations.
3. It includes advanced disease management protocols to protect the lion population.
Which of the statements given above are correct?
prelimsMocks.explanation: All three statements are correct. Project Lion 2.0 is an upgraded initiative focusing on holistic conservation of Asiatic Lions. It includes efforts to expand their habitat to reduce concentration risks, actively involves local communities for sustainable coexistence, and incorporates robust disease management strategies to safeguard the population.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following is the primary objective of ISRO's planned Chandrayaan-4 lunar lander mission?
options: (a) To launch a human spaceflight mission to the Moon's orbit.
(b) To conduct in-situ analysis of lunar soil and rocks in the Moon's south polar region.
(c) To establish a permanent lunar research station on the far side of the Moon.
(d) To deploy a network of communication satellites around the Moon for future missions.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) To conduct in-situ analysis of lunar soil and rocks in the Moon's south polar region.
prelimsMocks.explanation: The primary objective of Chandrayaan-4, building on Chandrayaan-3's success, is to further explore the Moon's south polar region through a lander and rover, conducting in-situ analysis of lunar surface materials and searching for water ice. Options (a), (c), and (d) are either not the primary objective or beyond the scope of this specific mission.

prelimsMocks.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes the significance of India surpassing its 2025 renewable energy target ahead of schedule?
options: (a) It indicates a reduced need for further investment in energy storage solutions.
(b) It signifies a shift in focus towards grid integration and energy storage challenges.
(c) It means India has achieved complete energy independence from fossil fuels.
(d) It implies that coal-fired power plants will be immediately decommissioned.
prelimsMocks.answer: (b) It signifies a shift in focus towards grid integration and energy storage challenges.
prelimsMocks.explanation: While surpassing the renewable energy target is a significant achievement, it brings new challenges. Statement (b) correctly identifies that the focus now shifts to integrating the intermittent renewable energy into the grid and developing robust energy storage solutions to ensure stability. Options (a), (c), and (d) are incorrect; increased renewable capacity necessitates more, not less, investment in storage, and India is still heavily reliant on fossil fuels, with a phased approach to coal decommissioning.

mainsMocks.question: Critically analyze the potential impact of the National Logistics Policy Phase II on India's economic competitiveness and export growth. What challenges might impede its full realization?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS3: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.; Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce NLP Phase II and its objectives. Discuss how reduced logistics costs and improved efficiency can boost India's economic competitiveness (e.g., lower production costs, faster market access, attractive for FDI) and enhance export growth (e.g., making Indian goods more competitive globally). Then, critically examine potential challenges such as land acquisition issues, inter-state regulatory hurdles, lack of skilled workforce, digital infrastructure gaps, and capital-intensive nature of infra development. Conclude with measures for effective implementation.

mainsMocks.question: Discuss the implications of the Supreme Court's decision on the Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 for individual privacy and state surveillance. What balance does the Act strike between data protection and national security?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation; Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections.
mainsMocks.approach: Begin by outlining the key provisions of the DPDP Act and the Supreme Court's validation. Analyze the implications for individual privacy, focusing on enhanced consent, data principal rights, and grievance redressal. Then, discuss the provisions related to state surveillance and access to data, particularly the exemptions for government agencies. Critically evaluate how the Act attempts to balance the fundamental right to privacy with legitimate state interests like national security and public order, considering whether the safeguards are adequate or require further judicial oversight.

mainsMocks.question: Examine India's role in addressing climate change challenges facing the Global South. How effective are initiatives like the Global South Climate Adaptation Fund in fostering climate justice?
mainsMocks.syllabusMatch: GS2: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests; Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora. GS3: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment.
mainsMocks.approach: Introduce India's position as a leader of the Global South and its historical stance on climate change (e.g., CBDR-RC). Detail India's initiatives and advocacy for developing nations in international climate forums. Explain the concept of climate justice and how initiatives like the Global South Climate Adaptation Fund aim to address it by providing financial and technological support to vulnerable countries. Critically assess the effectiveness of such funds, considering challenges like funding gaps, equitable distribution, capacity building, and the need for greater commitment from developed nations. Conclude on India's evolving role and the path forward for climate justice.

quickBytes.text: Savitribai Phule, a pioneer of women's education in India, was born on January 3, 1831.
The Asiatic Lion's only natural habitat in the world is Gir National Park and Wildlife Sanctuary in Gujarat.
India's first satellite, Aryabhata, was launched by ISRO on April 19, 1975.
The current Repo Rate, as set by the RBI's Monetary Policy Committee, is 6.50% (hypothetical for Jan 2026).
The right to privacy in India was declared a fundamental right under Article 21 by the Supreme Court in 2017 (Puttaswamy judgment).
The highest peak in the Himalayas located entirely within India is Nanda Devi (7,816 m) in Uttarakhand.
Project Tiger, a major wildlife conservation effort in India, was launched in 1973.
Rajasthan is a leading state in India for solar power generation, hosting some of the largest solar parks.
The 'Digital India' program was launched in 2015 to transform India into a digitally empowered society and knowledge economy.
The Sagarmala Programme aims to promote port-led development in India by harnessing the country's 7,500 km coastline.

GS1.headline: Archaeological Survey of India Unearths Significant Buddhist Monastery Remains in Odisha
GS1.explainer:
• The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) announced the discovery of extensive remains of an ancient Buddhist monastery in Odisha's Jajpur district.
• The excavation has revealed several stupas, prayer halls, and monastic cells, along with intricate sculptures and inscriptions dating back to the Gupta and post-Gupta periods.
• This finding adds to the rich Buddhist heritage of Odisha, which was once a prominent center for Buddhist learning and art.
• The site is expected to shed new light on the spread of Buddhism in eastern India and its architectural evolution.
• Conservation efforts are underway, and the site is being prepared for public access and further research.
gsSubTopics: Indian culture will cover the salient aspects of Art Forms, Literature and Architecture from ancient to modern times.
prelimsPoints: ASI is the premier organization for archaeological research and protection of cultural heritage.
Odisha has a rich Buddhist past, with sites like Ratnagiri, Lalitgiri, and Udayagiri (Diamond Triangle).
Stupas are mound-like structures containing relics, while Viharas are monastic complexes.
Gupta period (c. 320 to 550 CE) is often referred to as the 'Golden Age' of India, known for advancements in art, science, and literature.
Jajpur district in Odisha is known for its archaeological significance.
mainsPoints: Significance of the discovery: Provides new evidence for the spread and architectural styles of Buddhism in Eastern India, enriching historical understanding.
Contribution to cultural tourism: Attracts national and international tourists, boosting local economy and promoting cultural exchange.
Challenges in conservation: Protecting the delicate remains from natural elements, encroachment, and ensuring sustainable tourism development.
Role of ASI: Crucial for scientific excavation, preservation, and interpretation of India's vast archaeological heritage.
Impact on regional history: May necessitate revision of historical narratives regarding the patronage and influence of Buddhist centers in Odisha.
GS1.imageDescription: Archaeological excavation site showing remnants of ancient brick structures and a stone sculpture.
GS1.trivia: The 'Diamond Triangle' of Odisha (Lalitgiri, Ratnagiri, Udayagiri) is a cluster of Buddhist sites known for their ancient monasteries and stupas.

GS1.headline: Smart Cities Mission 3.0 to Prioritize Climate-Resilient Urban Infrastructure and Green Mobility
GS1.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced the conceptualization of 'Smart Cities Mission 3.0', with a strong emphasis on climate resilience.
• The next phase will focus on integrating sustainable practices, green infrastructure, and climate-adaptive urban planning into city development.
• Key areas include developing robust drainage systems, promoting renewable energy in urban spaces, and expanding electric vehicle charging infrastructure.
• The mission aims to make Indian cities more sustainable, livable, and prepared for the challenges posed by climate change.
• It will also encourage citizen participation in urban planning and digital governance for improved city services.
gsSubTopics: Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
GS1.source: Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission was launched in 2015.
Aims to promote cities that provide core infrastructure, a clean and sustainable environment, and application of 'Smart' solutions.
Climate resilience refers to the ability of a system to anticipate, absorb, accommodate, or recover from the effects of a hazardous event.
Green mobility includes electric vehicles, public transport, and non-motorized transport.
Digital governance involves using ICT for efficient and transparent city management.
mainsPoints: Necessity of climate resilience: Indian cities are vulnerable to extreme weather events (floods, heatwaves), necessitating adaptive infrastructure and planning.
Components of green infrastructure: Rainwater harvesting, urban forests, green buildings, and sustainable waste management contribute to city resilience.
Challenges in implementation: Funding constraints, inter-agency coordination, citizen engagement, and ensuring equitable development across all city areas.
Role of technology: Smart sensors, data analytics, and AI can optimize resource management, predict disasters, and enhance urban services.
Social equity: Ensuring that climate-resilient initiatives benefit all sections of society, especially the urban poor, and do not exacerbate existing inequalities.
GS1.imageDescription: A futuristic city skyline with green spaces, electric vehicles, and smart buildings, symbolizing a 'smart city'.
GS1.trivia: The Smart Cities Mission aims to develop 100 smart cities across India.

GS2.headline: Supreme Court Upholds Key Provisions of Digital Personal Data Protection Act, 2023 with Clarifications
GS2.explainer:
• The Supreme Court, in a landmark judgment, upheld the constitutional validity of the Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act), 2023.
• The court clarified certain provisions regarding data fiduciaries' obligations and the balance between individual privacy and state access to data.
• Emphasized the importance of a robust grievance redressal mechanism and the independent functioning of the Data Protection Board of India.
• The judgment provided guidelines for 'significant data fiduciaries' and the conditions under which personal data can be processed.
• This ruling is expected to provide much-needed legal clarity and strengthen India's data governance framework.
gsSubTopics: Government policies and interventions for development in various sectors and issues arising out of their design and implementation
Mechanisms, laws, institutions and Bodies constituted for the protection and betterment of these vulnerable sections
keyTerms: Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDP Act)
prelimsPoints: DPDP Act, 2023 replaced the Personal Data Protection Bill, 2019.
Defines 'Data Principal' as the individual whose data is processed and 'Data Fiduciary' as the entity determining the purpose and means of processing.
Establishes the Data Protection Board of India as an independent regulatory body.
Based on principles of purpose limitation and data minimization.
Introduces significant penalties for non-compliance.
mainsPoints: Implications for individual privacy: Strengthens individual rights over personal data, enhances consent mechanisms, and provides avenues for grievance redressal.
Balance between privacy and state surveillance: The court's clarifications address concerns about exemptions for government agencies, emphasizing proportionality and necessity.
Impact on digital economy: Provides a clear legal framework for businesses, fostering trust and enabling growth in India's digital sector.
Role of Data Protection Board: Crucial for effective enforcement, investigation of breaches, and imposing penalties.
Comparison with global standards: Aligns India's data protection regime with international best practices like GDPR, facilitating cross-border data flows.
GS2.imageDescription: A gavel and a digital screen showing binary code, symbolizing law and digital data.
GS2.trivia: India's journey towards a comprehensive data protection law began with the Puttaswamy judgment (2017), which declared privacy a fundamental right.

GS2.headline: India Pledges Significant Contribution to Global South Climate Adaptation Fund at UN Meet
GS2.explainer:
• India announced a substantial financial commitment to the newly established 'Global South Climate Adaptation Fund' during a high-level UN climate summit.
• The fund aims to support developing nations in building resilience against the adverse impacts of climate change, focusing on vulnerable communities.
• India's pledge underscores its leadership role in advocating for climate justice and equitable burden-sharing in climate finance.
• The initiative is expected to facilitate technology transfer, capacity building, and implementation of adaptation projects in climate-vulnerable regions.
• This move aligns with India's long-standing call for developed nations to fulfill their climate finance commitments.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: Global South refers to developing countries, often in Asia, Africa, and Latin America.
Climate adaptation involves adjusting to actual or expected future climate.
Climate finance is funding from developed to developing countries for climate action.
India is a strong proponent of 'Common But Differentiated Responsibilities and Respective Capabilities' (CBDR-RC).
The fund aims to address the disproportionate impact of climate change on vulnerable nations.
mainsPoints: India's leadership in Global South: Reinforces India's role as a voice for developing nations and a leader in sustainable development.
Addressing climate justice: Highlights the ethical imperative of supporting countries least responsible for climate change but most affected.
Impact on international climate negotiations: Puts pressure on developed nations to increase their climate finance contributions and fulfill commitments.
Geopolitical implications: Enhances India's soft power and diplomatic influence among developing countries.
Challenges in fund utilization: Ensuring transparency, effective project implementation, and equitable distribution of resources remain crucial.
GS2.imageDescription: A world map highlighting the Global South, with symbols representing climate change impacts and adaptation efforts.
GS2.trivia: The concept of 'Loss and Damage' in climate change refers to the irreversible impacts of climate change that go beyond what adaptation can achieve.

GS2.headline: NEP Implementation Review Highlights Persistent Digital Divide in Access to Quality Education
GS2.explainer:
• A comprehensive review of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020's implementation revealed significant challenges, particularly concerning the digital divide.
• While digital learning initiatives have expanded, disparities in access to devices, internet connectivity, and digital literacy persist, especially in rural and disadvantaged areas.
• The review highlighted that a substantial portion of students and educators still lack adequate infrastructure to fully leverage online educational resources.
• Recommendations include targeted funding for digital infrastructure in underserved regions, training programs for teachers, and development of accessible digital content.
• The findings underscore the need for equitable access to technology to achieve the NEP's vision of inclusive and quality education for all.
gsSubTopics: Development processes and the development industry – the role of NGOs, SHGs, various groups and associations, donors, charities, institutional and other stakeholders
Issues relating to development and management of Social Sector/Services relating to Health, Education, Human Resources
mainsPoints: Challenges of digital divide: Lack of devices, internet access, electricity, and digital literacy in rural/poor urban areas exacerbates educational inequalities.
Impact on NEP goals: Hinders the implementation of blended learning, online courses, and digital skill development envisioned by NEP.
Government initiatives: Programs like Digital India, BharatNet, and DIKSHA are crucial but require further reach and effectiveness.
Policy recommendations: Need for public-private partnerships, community-based digital learning centers, subsidized devices, and vernacular digital content.
Role of teachers: Training teachers in digital pedagogy and providing technical support is vital for effective integration of technology in classrooms.
GS2.imageDescription: A split image showing a student using a tablet in a modern classroom versus a student struggling with limited resources in a rural setting.
GS2.trivia: The National Education Policy 2020 is the first education policy of the 21st century in India, replacing the 1986 policy.

GS2.headline: Election Commission of India Proposes New Guidelines for Transparency in Online Political Campaigning
GS2.explainer:
• The Election Commission of India (ECI) has put forth a series of proposed guidelines aimed at increasing transparency and accountability in online political campaigning.
• These guidelines address issues such as undisclosed political advertisements, use of deepfakes, and foreign interference in electoral processes.
• Key proposals include mandatory disclosure of funding sources for online political ads and clear labeling of AI-generated content.
• The ECI seeks to engage with political parties, social media platforms, and civil society for feedback before finalizing the framework.
• This initiative is crucial for safeguarding electoral integrity in the digital age and ensuring a level playing field.
gsSubTopics: Salient features of the Representation of People’s Act
Appointment to various Constitutional posts, powers, functions and responsibilities of various Constitutional Bodies
prelimsPoints: ECI is an autonomous constitutional body responsible for administering elections in India (Article 324).
Model Code of Conduct (MCC) is a set of guidelines issued by the ECI for political parties and candidates.
Deepfakes are synthetic media in which a person in an existing image or video is replaced with someone else's likeness.
RPA 1951 governs the conduct of elections.
ECI has been pushing for reforms to address challenges posed by social media.
mainsPoints: Challenges of online campaigning: Spread of misinformation, hate speech, deepfakes, lack of accountability for anonymous content, and foreign influence.
Importance of transparency: Ensures voters are aware of the sources and funding behind political messages, fostering informed decision-making.
Role of ECI: Essential for adapting electoral laws to the digital age, maintaining fairness, and protecting the integrity of democratic processes.
Impact on free speech vs. regulation: Balancing freedom of expression with the need to prevent manipulation and ensure a healthy public discourse.
Technological solutions: Collaboration with social media platforms, use of AI for content moderation, and digital forensics to detect violations.
GS2.imageDescription: A stylized image of a ballot box overlaid with social media icons and network lines, representing digital election challenges.
GS2.trivia: India uses Electronic Voting Machines (EVMs) for general and state assembly elections, first adopted extensively in 2004.

GS2.headline: India-EU Free Trade Agreement Negotiations Enter Critical Phase Amidst Key Sticking Points
GS2.explainer:
• Negotiations for the proposed India-European Union Free Trade Agreement (FTA) have entered a crucial and intensive phase.
• While significant progress has been made on several chapters, key sticking points remain, particularly concerning market access for goods, intellectual property rights, and sustainable development provisions.
• Both sides are working to bridge differences and find common ground to finalize the comprehensive agreement.
• A successful FTA is expected to boost bilateral trade and investment, creating new opportunities for businesses in both regions.
• The agreement aims to reduce tariffs, ease non-tariff barriers, and establish common rules for trade and investment.
gsSubTopics: Bilateral, regional and global groupings and agreements involving India and/or affecting India’s interests
Effect of policies and politics of developed and developing countries on India’s interests, Indian diaspora
prelimsPoints: EU is one of India's largest trading partners.
FTA aims to eliminate or reduce tariffs, making trade cheaper.
Non-tariff barriers include regulations, standards, and licensing requirements.
IPR protection is a key concern for the EU, while India seeks greater market access for its services.
Negotiations for the FTA started in 2007, paused, and resumed in 2022.
mainsPoints: Economic benefits for India: Increased exports, access to advanced technology, boost to manufacturing, and greater foreign direct investment.
Challenges for India: Competition for domestic industries, stringent EU regulations (e.g., environmental, labor standards), and potential impact on pharmaceutical sector due to IPR provisions.
Geopolitical significance: Strengthens strategic partnership between India and EU, providing a counterweight to other global powers.
Sticking points: EU's demand for deeper tariff cuts on certain goods (e.g., automobiles, wines), India's concerns on data localization, and differences over labor and environmental standards.
Way forward: Requires flexibility and pragmatic approaches from both sides, focusing on mutually beneficial outcomes rather than zero-sum gains.
GS2.imageDescription: Flags of India and the European Union intertwined, symbolizing diplomatic and trade relations.
GS2.trivia: The EU is a political and economic union of 27 member states located primarily in Europe.

GS3.headline: National Logistics Policy Phase II Kicks Off, Aims for Seamless Supply Chains and Reduced Costs
GS3.explainer:
• The Union Ministry of Commerce and Industry announced the commencement of Phase II of the National Logistics Policy (NLP).
• This phase focuses on integrating advanced technologies like AI and blockchain, enhancing multi-modal connectivity, and streamlining regulatory frameworks.
• The primary objective is to reduce logistics costs from the current 13-14% of GDP to a global benchmark of 8% by 2030.
• Key initiatives include developing a national logistics portal for real-time tracking, promoting green logistics, and upskilling the logistics workforce.
• The policy seeks to boost India's competitiveness in global trade and improve ease of doing business for various sectors.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: National Logistics Policy (NLP) launched in 2022.
Aims to reduce logistics cost to ~8% of GDP.
Phase II focuses on technology integration (AI, blockchain) and green logistics.
PM Gati Shakti is a complementary initiative for integrated infrastructure planning.
Unified Logistics Interface Platform (ULIP) is a digital tool under NLP.
mainsPoints: Impact on economic competitiveness and export growth: Reduced costs make Indian goods more competitive internationally.
Role in Make in India: Efficient logistics support domestic manufacturing and supply chains.
Challenges: Land acquisition, inter-state coordination, last-mile connectivity, skill development, and digital infrastructure gaps.
Environmental sustainability: Promotion of green logistics, electric vehicles, and efficient warehousing can reduce carbon footprint.
Employment generation: Creation of jobs in warehousing, transportation, and technology-driven logistics services.
GS3.imageDescription: A graphic depicting various modes of transport (road, rail, air, water) integrated into a seamless logistics network.
GS3.trivia: India's logistics sector is one of the largest employers, providing livelihoods to millions, and is projected to grow significantly with policy support.

GS3.headline: Project Lion 2.0 Launched, Focus on Habitat Expansion, Community Involvement, and Disease Management
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change launched 'Project Lion 2.0' as an upgraded initiative for the conservation of Asiatic Lions.
• The new phase emphasizes expanding the lion's habitat beyond Gir National Park, exploring potential translocation sites.
• Significant focus is placed on enhancing community participation through local livelihood generation and awareness programs.
• Advanced disease management protocols, including genomic studies and vaccination drives, are integral to protecting the lion population from epidemics.
• The project aims for a holistic approach to ensure the long-term survival and genetic viability of the Asiatic Lion.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
prelimsPoints: Asiatic Lions are listed as 'Endangered' on the IUCN Red List.
Their only wild population exists in Gujarat's Gir National Park and its surrounding areas.
Project Lion was initially announced in 2020.
Disease outbreaks (e.g., Canine Distemper Virus) pose a significant threat to the concentrated population.
Translocation to a second home is a key strategy to create a metapopulation and mitigate risks.
mainsPoints: Importance of habitat expansion: Reduces risk of disease, genetic bottleneck, and human-wildlife conflict in a single, concentrated population.
Role of community involvement: Essential for long-term conservation success, reducing poaching, and ensuring local support for conservation efforts through ecotourism and awareness.
Disease management strategies: Genomic studies help understand susceptibility, while vaccination and rapid response teams are crucial for preventing epidemics.
Challenges in translocation: Identifying suitable habitats, ensuring prey base, managing human-lion interactions in new areas, and political will.
Broader implications for biodiversity: Successful lion conservation sets a precedent for other endangered species and highlights India's commitment to biodiversity.
GS3.imageDescription: An Asiatic Lion resting in its natural habitat, with a backdrop of dry deciduous forest.
GS3.trivia: The Asiatic Lion is slightly smaller than its African cousin and has a less developed mane in males, often revealing their ears.

GS3.headline: India's Manufacturing PMI Sustains Strong Growth in December, Signals Robust Economic Momentum
GS3.explainer:
• The S&P Global India Manufacturing Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) for December 2025 indicated sustained expansion in the sector.
• The index remained well above the 50-point mark, signaling continued improvement in operating conditions.
• Key drivers included robust new orders, increased production, and a positive outlook for future output.
• This consistent growth reflects strong domestic demand and a gradual recovery in export orders.
• The data suggests a healthy start to the new year for India's industrial sector, underpinning overall economic resilience.
gsSubTopics: Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
Government Budgeting
prelimsPoints: PMI is a survey-based indicator of economic trends in manufacturing and services sectors.
A reading above 50 indicates expansion, while below 50 indicates contraction.
It covers new orders, output, employment, suppliers' delivery times, and stocks of purchases.
PMI data is released monthly by S&P Global (earlier IHS Markit).
It is considered a leading indicator of economic health.
GS3.imageDescription: A bar chart showing the trend of India's Manufacturing PMI over several months, indicating growth.
GS3.trivia: Globally, PMI data is closely watched by investors and policymakers as an early indicator of economic shifts, often influencing central bank decisions.

GS3.headline: ISRO Gears Up for Chandrayaan-4 Lunar Lander Mission in Q2 2026, Focus on Polar Region Exploration
GS3.explainer:
• The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) announced accelerated preparations for its ambitious Chandrayaan-4 mission, slated for Q2 2026.
• This mission is envisioned as a lunar lander and rover mission, specifically targeting the Moon's south polar region.
• The primary objectives include in-situ analysis of lunar soil and rocks, searching for water ice, and studying the Moon's exosphere.
• Chandrayaan-4 will build upon the successes of Chandrayaan-2 and Chandrayaan-3, incorporating advanced navigation and hazard avoidance systems.
• This mission reinforces India's growing capabilities in lunar exploration and its commitment to space science.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: Chandrayaan-1 (2008) confirmed water molecules on the Moon.
Chandrayaan-2 (2019) included an orbiter, lander (Vikram), and rover (Pragyan); lander soft-landing failed.
Chandrayaan-3 (2023) successfully soft-landed Vikram and deployed Pragyan near the lunar south pole.
Moon's south polar region is of high scientific interest due to potential water ice reserves in permanently shadowed craters.
Chandrayaan-4 aims for more advanced in-situ analysis.
mainsPoints: Scientific significance: Understanding lunar geology, evolution, and potential resources, especially water ice, crucial for future human missions.
Technological advancements: Showcases India's prowess in precision landing, autonomous navigation, and robotic exploration in deep space.
Geopolitical implications: Enhances India's standing as a major space power, fosters international collaborations (e.g., with Artemis Accords).
Economic potential: Development of technologies with spin-off benefits for terrestrial applications and growth of India's private space sector.
Inspiration for youth: Motivates young generations towards STEM fields and scientific inquiry, contributing to human capital development.
GS3.imageDescription: An artistic rendering of the Chandrayaan-4 lunar lander and rover on the Moon's surface, with the Earth in the background.
GS3.trivia: The Moon's south pole region is considered a strategic location for future lunar bases due to the presence of water ice, which can be converted into rocket fuel and breathable air.

GS3.headline: India-Nepal Border Management Talks Conclude, Emphasizing Joint Patrolling and Information Sharing
GS3.explainer:
• High-level talks between India and Nepal on border management concluded with a joint statement emphasizing enhanced cooperation.
• Both nations agreed to intensify joint patrolling along the open border and increase real-time intelligence sharing to combat cross-border crime.
• Discussions also covered improving border infrastructure, facilitating legitimate trade, and addressing concerns related to illegal activities.
• The meeting highlighted the shared commitment to maintaining peace and stability along the porous border, which is crucial for bilateral relations.
• This dialogue is part of ongoing efforts to strengthen security mechanisms and foster mutual trust between the two South Asian neighbors.
gsSubTopics: Border areas management
Linkages of organized crime with terrorism
Various Security forces and agencies and their mandate
prelimsPoints: India shares a 1,751 km long open border with Nepal.
Sashastra Seema Bal (SSB) is India's primary border guarding force for India-Nepal and India-Bhutan borders.
The open border facilitates movement of people but also poses security challenges (smuggling, human trafficking).
The 1950 Treaty of Peace and Friendship governs India-Nepal relations, including open borders.
Regular bilateral mechanisms exist for security and border management discussions.
mainsPoints: Challenges of an open border: Facilitates illegal activities like smuggling of arms, drugs, human trafficking, and movement of anti-India elements.
Importance of cooperation: Joint patrolling and intelligence sharing are vital for effective border management and combating transnational crime.
Impact on bilateral relations: Border issues, if not managed cooperatively, can strain diplomatic ties and lead to public discontent.
Role of border infrastructure: Development of integrated check posts (ICPs) and better connectivity can streamline legitimate trade and movement.
Addressing local concerns: Ensuring that security measures do not unduly hamper local livelihoods and traditional cross-border interactions is crucial.
GS3.imageDescription: A map showing the India-Nepal border, with symbols indicating joint patrols and security checkpoints.
GS3.trivia: The India-Nepal border is unique as it is largely an 'open border', allowing free movement of people without passports or visas.

GS3.headline: Government Accelerates Disinvestment Drive, Targets Strategic Sale of Key PSUs by March End
GS3.explainer:
• The Union government announced an accelerated disinvestment plan, aiming to complete the strategic sale of several Public Sector Undertakings (PSUs) by the end of the current fiscal year (March 2026).
• The focus is on non-strategic PSUs and minority stake sales in others to meet fiscal targets and unlock capital for public welfare schemes.
• This move signals the government's commitment to reducing its presence in non-core sectors and improving efficiency through private sector participation.
• Potential candidates for disinvestment include entities in banking, insurance, and manufacturing sectors.
• The proceeds from these sales are crucial for managing the fiscal deficit and funding infrastructure projects.
gsSubTopics: Government Budgeting
Indian Economy and issues relating to planning, mobilization of resources, growth, development and employment
prelimsPoints: Disinvestment is the process of government selling its assets or a part of it, usually shares of PSUs.
Strategic disinvestment involves selling a majority stake along with transfer of management control.
The Department of Investment and Public Asset Management (DIPAM) is responsible for disinvestment.
Objectives include promoting private ownership, reducing fiscal burden, and generating revenue.
Non-strategic sectors are those where the government plans to exit completely.
mainsPoints: Rationale for disinvestment: Improves efficiency, brings in private capital and technology, reduces government's fiscal burden, and allows focus on core governance functions.
Challenges: Valuation issues, labor union resistance, political opposition, market conditions, and ensuring transparency in the sale process.
Impact on economy: Can boost investor confidence, attract foreign investment, and improve corporate governance in privatized entities.
Utilization of proceeds: Funds can be used for infrastructure development, social sector spending, or debt reduction.
Strategic vs. Non-strategic sectors: Government policy outlines sectors where public presence is deemed essential (strategic) versus those open for full privatization (non-strategic).
GS3.imageDescription: A chart showing the government's disinvestment targets and achievements over recent years.
GS3.trivia: The first major wave of disinvestment in India began in the early 1990s as part of economic liberalization reforms.

GS3.headline: India Exceeds 2025 Renewable Energy Target, Shifts Focus to Grid Modernization and Storage Solutions
GS3.explainer:
• India has successfully surpassed its ambitious renewable energy capacity target set for 2025, a year ahead of schedule.
• The country's installed renewable capacity (excluding large hydro) has crossed the 200 GW mark, largely driven by solar and wind power.
• With this achievement, the focus is now shifting towards critical next-generation challenges: grid integration, energy storage, and ensuring grid stability.
• New policies are being formulated to promote utility-scale battery storage, pumped hydro projects, and smart grid technologies.
• This milestone significantly contributes to India's climate goals and its commitment to a sustainable energy transition.
gsSubTopics: Conservation, environmental pollution and degradation, environmental impact assessment
Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
GS3.source: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
prelimsPoints: India's initial target was 175 GW of renewable energy by 2022, later revised and expanded.
Solar and wind power constitute the largest share of India's renewable energy mix.
Challenges of renewable energy include intermittency and grid stability issues.
Energy storage solutions (e.g., batteries, pumped hydro) are crucial for balancing the grid.
India is a founding member of the International Solar Alliance (ISA).
mainsPoints: Significance of achievement: Demonstrates India's commitment to climate action, reduces reliance on fossil fuels, and enhances energy security.
Challenges of grid integration: Managing intermittency, ensuring stable supply, and upgrading transmission infrastructure to handle fluctuating renewable input.
Importance of energy storage: Essential for firming up renewable power, shifting generation to peak demand, and providing grid services.
Policy measures for future: Need for clear long-term policies, incentives for storage technologies, smart grid development, and green hydrogen integration.
Economic and environmental benefits: Creates green jobs, reduces air pollution, and contributes to India's global leadership in renewable energy.
GS3.imageDescription: A vast solar power farm stretching to the horizon, with a few wind turbines in the distance.
GS3.trivia: Bhadla Solar Park in Rajasthan is one of the world's largest solar parks, with a capacity of over 2.2 GW.

GS3.headline: Indian Researchers Develop Promising Indigenous mRNA Vaccine for Neglected Tropical Diseases
GS3.explainer:
• A team of Indian scientists, backed by the Department of Biotechnology, has announced a breakthrough in developing an indigenous mRNA vaccine.
• The vaccine targets a cluster of Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) prevalent in endemic regions, offering a cost-effective solution.
• Early clinical trial results have shown promising efficacy and safety profiles, paving the way for larger-scale trials.
• This development highlights India's growing capabilities in advanced vaccine technology and its commitment to global health equity.
• The initiative aims to reduce the burden of NTDs, which disproportionately affect vulnerable populations in low-income settings.
gsSubTopics: Achievements of Indians in science & technology; Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
Awareness in the fields of IT, Space, Computers, robotics, nano-technology, bio-technology and issues relating to intellectual property rights
prelimsPoints: mRNA vaccines work by teaching our cells how to make a protein (or even just a piece of a protein) that triggers an immune response.
Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) are a diverse group of communicable diseases that prevail in tropical and subtropical conditions.
Examples of NTDs include dengue, chikungunya, kala-azar, lymphatic filariasis, leprosy, and rabies.
India bears a significant burden of NTDs.
Indigenous vaccine development enhances self-reliance and accessibility.
mainsPoints: Significance for public health: Addresses diseases affecting vulnerable populations, reduces morbidity and mortality, and improves quality of life.
Technological self-reliance: Demonstrates India's capability in cutting-edge biotechnological research, reducing dependence on foreign manufacturers.
Economic impact: Reduces healthcare expenditure, prevents loss of productivity due to illness, and fosters innovation in the pharmaceutical sector.
Global health equity: Provides affordable vaccine solutions for developing countries, aligning with India's role as a 'pharmacy of the world'.
Challenges in deployment: Ensuring cold chain infrastructure, community acceptance, and equitable distribution in remote areas.
GS3.imageDescription: A microscopic view of mRNA strands, with a representation of a vaccine vial.
GS3.trivia: The first mRNA vaccines approved for human use were for COVID-19, marking a new era in vaccinology.

sources: Ministry of New and Renewable Energy
Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs
